one_line_takeaway: Forget-MI unlearns both unimodal and cross-modal patient representations from a multimodal (X-ray + report) medical model via four weighted embedding-distance losses plus noise, cutting membership-inference attack success below all baselines while matching the retrained model's test accuracy -- though its advantage shrinks for larger deletion requests.
key_finding: Forget-MI achieves the lowest MIA among all baselines while keeping test-set performance close to the retrained reference. On forgetting 3%, Forget-MI reaches MIA 0.571 vs MultiDelete 0.806 and vs 1.000 for all modality-agnostic baselines (NegGrad+, SCRUB, CF-k, EU-k); Df AUC drops to 0.735 (vs 0.858 MultiDelete, 0.999 original) and Df F1 to 0.393, while Dt AUC 0.625 stays near the retrained model's 0.626. The paper's headline aggregate claim: Forget-MI reduces MIA by 0.202 and cuts forget-set AUC and F1 by 0.221 and 0.305 respectively. Noise is essential: noiseless runs collapse (No Noise config gives MIA 1.000 with Dt AUC ~0.51, near chance). Modality-aware methods (Forget-MI, MultiDelete) push the model meaningfully farther from the original toward the retrained model (avg distance 0.483/0.410/0.561 for 3/6/10% vs original's 0.251 and agnostic baselines' 0.323), whereas modality-agnostic baselines barely move.
core_contribution: Forget-MI is an approximate machine-unlearning method for multimodal medical models that erases BOTH unimodal and joint (cross-modal) representations of the data a patient asks to have removed, while preserving performance on retained data. Its novelty relative to the cited MultiDelete method (Cheng & Amiri, 2024), which the authors explicitly build on and contrast against, is that MultiDelete deliberately retains unimodal knowledge of the forget set and only breaks the cross-modal association; the authors argue that retaining unimodal patient information is unacceptable in healthcare, so Forget-MI unlearns at both levels. Concretely, the contribution is a system of four weighted loss functions (unimodal unlearning, multimodal unlearning, unimodal retention, multimodal retention) combined with modality-specific noise perturbation of the forget set to force forgetting of a neighborhood around each forgotten point rather than isolated points. The authors also claim to be the first to handle multimodal unlearning in healthcare while accounting for multiple studies per patient.
threat_model: Privacy adversary enforcing the legal 'right to be forgotten'. The concrete attacker is a black-box Membership Inference Attack (MIA): the adversary sees only model outputs/losses and trains an SVM classifier (following Kurmanji et al.) to distinguish forget-set samples from test-set samples, i.e., to determine whether a specific patient's data was used in training. Successful unlearning means the attacker can no longer tell forgotten data apart from unseen data (MIA -> 0.5 ideal, lower is better). The paper frames this in the healthcare context where retention of sensitive patient imaging/text after a withdrawal request is the harm being defended against.
limitations: (1) Unlearning degrades as the removal request grows: at 10% forgetting Forget-MI's MIA is only 0.810 -- still far above the retrained model's 0.190 -- so it does not reach the exact-unlearning ideal for large requests; the authors concede current algorithms have inherent limits at scale. (2) MultiDelete attains higher test/forget F1 because Forget-MI's stronger forgetting sacrifices the low-represented edema classes in an imbalanced 4-class problem. (3) Runtime overhead: Forget-MI takes up to ~5 hours vs ~4 hours for the agnostic baselines (though far below the 14-hour full retrain). (4) The retain set is artificially fixed to the forget-set size each epoch rather than using all of Dr, a simplification forced by the assumption that the full training data may be unavailable. (5) Single dataset (a MIMIC-CXR subset), single task (edema staging), two modalities only; generalization to other clinical tasks and foundation models is left to future work. (6) Loss weights are hand-tuned per forget percentage with no principled selection rule.

datasets_used: MIMIC-CXR subset (adopted from Chauhan et al. 2020): 6,742 chest X-ray images with paired radiology reports from 1,663 subjects
Task: 4-stage pulmonary edema classification -- no edema 43%, vascular congestion 25%, interstitial edema 22%, alveolar edema 10%
quantitative_results: Headline aggregate: reduces MIA by 0.202; reduces forget-set AUC by 0.221 and forget-set F1 by 0.305
3% forget -- Forget-MI: MIA 0.571, Df AUC 0.735, Df F1 0.393, Dt AUC 0.625, Dt F1 0.250
6% forget -- Forget-MI (Multimodal): MIA 0.615, Df AUC 0.654, Df F1 0.328, Dt AUC 0.599, Dt F1 0.270
10% forget -- Forget-MI (Retention): MIA 0.810, Df AUC 0.656, Df F1 0.313, Dt AUC 0.565, Dt F1 0.252
Reference Original model: MIA 1.000, Df AUC 0.999, Dt AUC 0.677
Reference Retrain model: MIA 0.000/0.769/0.190 and Dt AUC 0.626/0.702/0.629 at 3/6/10%
MultiDelete baseline: MIA 0.806/0.846/0.952 at 3/6/10% (best baseline, still worse MIA than Forget-MI)
Modality-agnostic baselines (NegGrad+, SCRUB, CF-k, EU-k): MIA 1.000 in essentially all settings
No-Noise ablation collapses: MIA 1.000, Dt AUC ~0.505-0.521 (near chance)
Distance to retrained model: Forget-MI 0.483/0.410/0.561 vs original 0.251 vs agnostic-baseline avg 0.323
Runtime: full retrain up to 14h; agnostic baselines within 4h; Forget-MI and MultiDelete up to 5h
baselines_compared: NegGrad+ (Kurmanji et al.) -- fine-tune on Dr while negating gradient on Df
SCRUB (Kurmanji et al.) -- teacher-student, student disobeys teacher on Df
CF-k (Goel et al.) -- freeze first k=2 layers, fine-tune rest on Dr
EU-k (Goel et al.) -- retrain unfrozen layers on Dr
MultiDelete (Cheng & Amiri) -- multimodal, unlearns Df only at cross-modal level, retains unimodal
Retrain (exact unlearning) and Original (no unlearning) as reference points
